stem: For insurance claim prediction, the base rate for a homeowner depends on which reference class is used: Reference Class A (all homeowners nationally, n=1000): base rate = 25%. Reference Class B (homeowners in this ZIP code, n=50): base rate = 47%. Reference Class C (homeowners with this specific home profile, n=30): base rate = 56%. Each reference class has a different level of specificity and sample size. What is the appropriate base rate to use, and how should the choice of reference class affect the uncertainty in the prediction?

ground_truth.answer: The reference class problem: different classes give rates of 25%, 47%, 56%. No single class is objectively 'correct'. Combined interval spanning all classes: [0.224, 0.721]. Weighted point estimate (specificity-weighted): 0.467.

solution_steps: Class A (all homeowners nationally): rate=0.25, n=1000, CI=[0.224, 0.278]. Large sample but least specific.
Class B (homeowners in this ZIP code): rate=0.47, n=50, CI=[0.339, 0.605]. Moderate specificity and sample size.
Class C (homeowners with this specific home profile): rate=0.56, n=30, CI=[0.386, 0.721]. Most specific but smallest sample (widest CI).
The reference class problem has no definitive solution — the 'correct' base rate depends on which class membership is considered most relevant.
Full uncertainty range spanning all reference classes: [0.224, 0.721].
common_failure_modes: Choosing one reference class without acknowledging the others
Using only the most specific class (Class C) despite its small sample size
Using only the broadest class (Class A) despite its low relevance
Not recognizing that the choice of reference class is itself a source of fundamental uncertainty
